title: Semantic HTML
question: What is semantic HTML and why does it matter?
answer: Semantic HTML uses elements whose names describe their meaning (header, nav, main, article, section, footer). It improves accessibility, SEO, and maintainability.
explanation: Screen readers and search engines use semantic tags to understand structure — divs and spans convey no meaning.
codeExample:
Title

Body
bestPractices: Use exactly one <main> per page.
commonMistakes: Wrapping everything in <div>s.
interviewTips: Mention landmark roles.

title: DOCTYPE
question: What is the purpose of <!DOCTYPE html>?
answer: It tells the browser to use standards mode instead of quirks mode. The simple HTML5 doctype is `<!DOCTYPE html>`.
explanation: Without it, browsers use legacy layout rules causing subtle bugs.
codeExample:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en"><head></head><body></body></html>
bestPractices: Always include it as the first line.
commonMistakes: Omitting doctype in copy-pasted templates.
interviewTips: Explain standards vs quirks mode.

title: Data Attributes
question: What are data-* attributes?
answer: Custom attributes prefixed with `data-` store extra info on elements, readable via dataset in JS.
explanation: Great for passing small values to scripts without polluting IDs/classes.
codeExample:
<button data-user-id="42">Edit</button>
// JS
el.dataset.userId // '42'
bestPractices: Keep values simple and small.
commonMistakes: Storing sensitive data in the DOM.
interviewTips: Explain dataset camelCasing.

title: Forms & Validation
question: How does HTML5 form validation work?
answer: Attributes like required, minlength, pattern, type=email add native validation without JS. The Constraint Validation API exposes checkValidity(), setCustomValidity().
explanation: Style invalid inputs with :invalid pseudo-class.
codeExample: <input type="email" required pattern=".+@.+\..+" />
bestPractices: Always validate again server-side.
commonMistakes: Relying only on client validation.
interviewTips: Show novalidate and custom messages.

title: Accessibility (ARIA)
question: When should you use ARIA attributes?
answer: Use native HTML first. ARIA fills gaps for custom widgets — e.g. role='dialog', aria-live, aria-expanded.
explanation: Misused ARIA can hurt accessibility more than help.
codeExample: <button aria-expanded="false" aria-controls="menu">Menu</button>
bestPractices: First rule of ARIA: don't use ARIA if a native element exists.
commonMistakes: Adding role=button to a div instead of using <button>.
interviewTips: Mention focus management for dialogs.

title: Meta Tags & SEO
question: Which meta tags matter most for SEO?
answer: The `<title>` and `<meta name="description">` are the primary SEO tags. `<meta name="robots">` controls indexing, and a `<link rel="canonical">` prevents duplicate-content penalties.
explanation: Search engines display title/description in results (SERP snippets). Keyword meta tags are ignored by Google. Canonical URLs consolidate ranking signals across duplicate pages.
codeExample:
<head>
  <title>SkillNest — HTML Interview Prep</title>
  <meta name="description" content="Master HTML interview questions." />
  <meta name="robots" content="index,follow" />
  <link rel="canonical" href="https://skillnest.dev/html" />
</head>
bestPractices: Keep titles under ~60 characters and descriptions under ~160.
Use one canonical URL per page.
commonMistakes: Relying on the deprecated keywords meta tag.
Duplicating the same title across many pages.
interviewTips: Explain the difference between crawling and indexing.

title: Open Graph
question: What are Open Graph meta tags used for?
answer: Open Graph (`og:`) tags control how a page appears when shared on social platforms like Facebook, LinkedIn, and Slack — defining the title, description, image, and URL of the preview card.
explanation: Without OG tags, platforms guess metadata inconsistently. Twitter uses its own `twitter:` card tags but falls back to Open Graph.
codeExample:
<meta property="og:title" content="SkillNest" />
<meta property="og:description" content="Interview prep made simple." />
<meta property="og:image" content="https://skillnest.dev/preview.png" />
<meta property="og:url" content="https://skillnest.dev" />
<meta name="twitter:card" content="summary_large_image" />
bestPractices: Provide an absolute URL for og:image (min 1200x630).
Include twitter:card for rich Twitter previews.
commonMistakes: Using relative image URLs that scrapers cannot resolve.
Forgetting to re-scrape after updating tags.
interviewTips: Mention platform debuggers for validating previews.

title: Responsive Images (srcset/picture)
question: How do srcset and <picture> serve responsive images?
answer: `srcset` with `sizes` lets the browser pick the best-resolution image for the viewport/DPR. `<picture>` with `<source>` enables art direction and modern-format fallbacks (WebP/AVIF).
explanation: srcset solves resolution switching (same image, different sizes). picture solves art direction (different crops) and format selection.
bestPractices: Always keep a plain with src as fallback.
Match sizes to your CSS layout breakpoints.
commonMistakes: Confusing resolution switching with art direction.
Omitting the sizes attribute so the browser assumes 100vw.
interviewTips: Explain w descriptors vs x descriptors.

title: Lazy Loading
question: How does native lazy loading work in HTML?
answer: Adding `loading="lazy"` to `` and `
explanation: The browser uses viewport proximity heuristics. Above-the-fold images should stay `loading="eager"` so they are not delayed.
bestPractices: Set explicit width/height to avoid layout shift (CLS).
Keep hero images eager, not lazy.
commonMistakes: Lazy-loading above-the-fold images and hurting LCP.
Omitting dimensions and causing cumulative layout shift.
interviewTips: Relate lazy loading to Core Web Vitals (LCP/CLS).

title: iframe & sandbox
question: What does the sandbox attribute on an iframe do?
answer: `sandbox` applies a restrictive security policy to embedded content — disabling scripts, forms, popups, and same-origin access by default. You re-enable capabilities selectively via tokens like `allow-scripts`.
explanation: Sandboxing isolates untrusted third-party embeds, mitigating XSS and clickjacking. An empty `sandbox` is the most restrictive.
codeExample:
<iframe src="https://widget.example.com"
        sandbox="allow-scripts allow-forms"
        title="Third-party widget"
        referrerpolicy="no-referrer"></iframe>
bestPractices: Grant the minimum tokens needed.
Avoid combining allow-scripts with allow-same-origin for untrusted sources.
commonMistakes: Using allow-scripts allow-same-origin together, letting the frame remove its own sandbox.
Embedding untrusted content without any sandbox.
interviewTips: Explain why allow-scripts + allow-same-origin defeats the sandbox.

title: script async vs defer
question: What is the difference between async and defer on <script>?
answer: Both download the script without blocking HTML parsing. `async` executes as soon as it is fetched (order not guaranteed); `defer` waits until parsing completes and preserves document order, running before DOMContentLoaded.
explanation: Use defer for scripts that depend on the DOM or each other; use async for independent scripts like analytics.
codeExample:
<script src="analytics.js" async></script>
<script src="app.js" defer></script>
<script src="utils.js" defer></script>
<!-- app.js runs before utils.js, both after parse -->
bestPractices: Prefer defer for application code so DOM is ready.
Use async for independent third-party scripts.
commonMistakes: Assuming async preserves execution order.
Placing render-blocking scripts in <head> without async/defer.
interviewTips: Sketch the parse/fetch/execute timeline for each.

title: localStorage vs sessionStorage vs cookies
question: How do localStorage, sessionStorage, and cookies differ?
answer: localStorage persists until cleared (~5MB, per-origin). sessionStorage lasts only for the tab session. Cookies (~4KB) are sent with every HTTP request and support server-side auth with HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite flags.
explanation: Web Storage is client-only and synchronous; cookies travel to the server. Choose cookies for auth tokens (HttpOnly), storage for UI state.
bestPractices: Store auth tokens in HttpOnly cookies, not localStorage.
Set SameSite and Secure on sensitive cookies.
commonMistakes: Keeping JWTs in localStorage where XSS can steal them.
Bloating cookies with large data sent on every request.
interviewTips: Discuss XSS vs CSRF trade-offs of each storage.

title: Web Components & Shadow DOM
question: What are custom elements and the shadow DOM?
answer: Web Components let you define reusable custom elements via `customElements.define`. The shadow DOM attaches an encapsulated subtree with scoped styles that do not leak in or out.
explanation: Shadow DOM provides style and markup isolation, enabling framework-agnostic, reusable widgets.
bestPractices: Use <slot> for composable content.
Name custom elements with a hyphen (required).
commonMistakes: Forgetting the required hyphen in the tag name.
Expecting global CSS to style inside an open shadow root.
interviewTips: Contrast open vs closed shadow root modes.

title: Canvas vs SVG
question: When should you use <canvas> versus <svg>?
answer: SVG is vector, DOM-based, and resolution-independent — ideal for icons, charts, and interactive shapes. Canvas is an immediate-mode pixel bitmap — better for high-performance rendering of many objects, games, and image manipulation.
explanation: SVG elements are individually inspectable/scriptable and accessible; canvas has no DOM per shape, so you manage state and redraws yourself.
codeExample:
<svg viewBox="0 0 100 100"><circle cx="50" cy="50" r="40" fill="teal"/></svg>

<canvas id="c" width="100" height="100"></canvas>
<script>
  const ctx = document.getElementById('c').getContext('2d');
  ctx.fillStyle = 'teal';
  ctx.beginPath();
  ctx.arc(50, 50, 40, 0, Math.PI * 2);
  ctx.fill();
</script>
bestPractices: Prefer SVG for scalable UI icons and accessibility.
Use canvas when rendering thousands of objects or per-pixel effects.
commonMistakes: Using canvas for simple static icons.
Expecting canvas content to be accessible without extra ARIA.
interviewTips: Explain retained-mode (SVG) vs immediate-mode (canvas).

title: Audio & Video
question: How do the <audio> and <video> elements work?
answer: They embed native media with a `controls` attribute for playback UI. Multiple `<source>` children provide format fallbacks, and `<track>` adds captions/subtitles for accessibility.
explanation: Attributes like autoplay, muted, loop, and preload tune behavior. Most browsers block autoplay unless muted.
codeExample:
<video controls width="640" preload="metadata" poster="cover.jpg">
  <source src="clip.webm" type="video/webm" />
  <source src="clip.mp4" type="video/mp4" />
  <track kind="captions" src="caps.vtt" srclang="en" label="English" default />
  Your browser does not support video.
</video>
bestPractices: Provide multiple formats for cross-browser support.
Always add <track> captions for accessibility.
commonMistakes: Expecting autoplay without the muted attribute.
Serving a single format unsupported by some browsers.
interviewTips: Mention preload=metadata to save bandwidth.

title: contenteditable
question: What does the contenteditable attribute do?
answer: `contenteditable="true"` makes any element user-editable in place, forming the basis of rich-text editors. The browser fires input events and manages the caret/selection.
explanation: It exposes a raw editing surface; production editors sanitize the resulting HTML and often use the Selection/Range APIs rather than the deprecated document.execCommand.
codeExample: Edit me...
bestPractices: Sanitize edited HTML before storing or rendering it.
Add role and aria-label for assistive tech.
commonMistakes: Trusting contenteditable output without sanitization (XSS risk).
Relying on the deprecated execCommand API.
interviewTips: Mention Selection and Range APIs for cursor control.

title: Input Types
question: Why use specialized HTML5 input types?
answer: Types like email, number, date, tel, url, color, and range trigger appropriate mobile keyboards, native pickers, and built-in validation — improving UX without extra JavaScript.
explanation: The right input type also enables autofill and constraint validation. Pair with inputmode and autocomplete for finer control.
codeExample:
<input type="email" autocomplete="email" required />
<input type="number" min="1" max="10" step="1" />
<input type="date" />
<input type="tel" inputmode="numeric" pattern="[0-9]{10}" />
bestPractices: Use type + inputmode to optimize mobile keyboards.
Add autocomplete tokens to speed up form filling.
commonMistakes: Using type=text for emails/numbers and losing native features.
Assuming date pickers render identically across browsers.
interviewTips: Explain inputmode vs type differences.

title: Accessibility Landmarks
question: What are ARIA/landmark regions and why do they help?
answer: Landmarks (banner, navigation, main, complementary, contentinfo) map to semantic elements like header, nav, main, aside, footer. Screen-reader users jump between them to navigate a page quickly.
explanation: Native semantic elements provide implicit landmark roles, so you rarely need explicit role attributes.
codeExample:
<header>Banner</header>
<nav aria-label="Primary">...</nav>
<main>
  <article>...</article>
</main>
<aside aria-label="Related">...</aside>
<footer>Contentinfo</footer>
bestPractices: Label multiple same-type landmarks with aria-label.
Use exactly one <main> per page.
commonMistakes: Adding redundant role attributes to semantic elements.
Having multiple unlabeled <nav> landmarks.
interviewTips: List which elements map to which landmark roles.

title: tabindex & Focus
question: How does the tabindex attribute affect focus order?
answer: `tabindex="0"` adds an element to the natural tab order; `tabindex="-1"` makes it programmatically focusable but skipped by tabbing; positive values force a custom order (an anti-pattern).
explanation: Keyboard accessibility relies on logical focus order. Prefer native focusable elements and DOM order over positive tabindex.
codeExample:
Custom button
Focused via JS: panel.focus()
bestPractices: Use tabindex=0 and -1 only; avoid positive values.
Manage focus on route changes and dialog open/close.
commonMistakes: Using positive tabindex and breaking natural order.
Making non-interactive text focusable.
interviewTips: Explain roving tabindex for composite widgets.

title: Meta Viewport
question: What does the viewport meta tag do?
answer: `<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">` tells mobile browsers to match the layout width to the device width instead of emulating a wide desktop, enabling responsive design.
explanation: Without it, phones render at ~980px and scale down, making text tiny. Avoid disabling user zoom for accessibility.
codeExample:
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
<!-- Avoid: user-scalable=no blocks pinch-zoom -->
bestPractices: Always include width=device-width, initial-scale=1.
Do not disable user zooming.
commonMistakes: Omitting the viewport tag on responsive sites.
Setting maximum-scale=1 or user-scalable=no.
interviewTips: Explain the default 980px viewport behavior.
